sentences.text: Oh, the bar mitzvah years ago was just you say the maftir you say.
And-- and you read in the Torah.
We used to-- I used to do that.
I did that.
And then you just make a little snack, cake, and a drink, wine, and a little herring and this and that, you know, little snacks.
That was the whole bar mitzvah.
Nothing elaborate.
Oh, I feel very happy.
I did a good job.
I had a good voice.
And I say it real loud.
And I have Torah.
And everybody was happy.
My mother for sure was very happy, uncles.
Oh, Jewish holidays was really-- was really beautiful.
The Jewish holidays was really nice, you know, when my uncle and my zaida were there always and we came Pesach.
Oh, he had a light-- a light, long garment, a light, long garment.
He had a beautiful tall, nice yarmulke, and was really very, very traditional, very good.
We all kids have to say it, and everybody loud, not move away from the table.
You'll sit and listen and say it and-- 'til it was finished.
And Sukkot also was a nice holiday, so we had a sukkah.
And-- but if it was pouring, we all went into the house.
My grandfather had a little something to put on just on top.
And my mother gave him all the food through the window [LAUGHS] in the sukkah.
Yeah.
That was interesting.
And then, my zayde used to make shmear for Pesach.
He used to make the [NON-ENGLISH].
That means very strictly.
That's every kernel-- this was from our farm.
Every kernel has to be picked.
All the kids were sitting by tables, pick out whatever is not clean, whatever was swollen, everything out, really [NON-ENGLISH].
My zayde, he had a mill, you know, to-- to make flour by hand, a round one, because usually when we have a lot, we used to go to the-- to one place where they have a big mill, you know.
But this, for Pesach, he used to cut out the stone, make it really for Pesach.
Cut out the chisel, cut out, clean it out, and then make it.
And he also gave to the rabbis in Krakowice and Jaworow, not to every-- every rabbi wouldn't take it, but from my grandfather, from my mother, whatever we brought them, they were happy.
We brought them the flour and they were making the matzohs, very, very strict, strict, really strict.
So-- And the holidays were really nice.
The holidays, everybody was really nice, enjoyable, family together.
[NON-ENGLISH] bringing presents, gifts, all kind of holidays.
Oh, this was already starting like 1930, '33 when Hitler came to power.
And they start putting out young boys, Ukrainian boys and also in Poland was the same thing, putting on placards putting on [NON-ENGLISH].
Hit the Jew, kill him.
A Jew is a pig, [NON-ENGLISH], all kind terrible things.
And then making swastikas, making on the walls.
Nobody did it by us, because they respected my mother on account of the parents.
This is the younger generation did it.
But it started getting bad, so everybody was thinking to go out, so I used to go to look first in Lemberg, to a big city, to find a job in the city.
Well, find a good job, and I had where to stay by a cousin.
And she had a fur-- a fur.
And they were dealing with furs.
They had a beautiful story in Lwow.
So I stayed there and I was looking around for a job, but I couldn't get it.
But then I came back home and I went to Krakow, Poland.
We-- I went there.
I had a cousin there.
He had a restaurant.
This was 19-- it's starting 1933, going, you know, looking for a job.
And then I came 1935, the end of '34, I came to Krakow and I stayed by my cousin.
And he had another room where to sleep.
And also, I had already my sister was already in Krakow.
She was working for a wholesale.
They were selling materials for clothes for ladies, special for ladies clothes.
So she worked there in the office.
My sister Pepka, the second-- the second sister.
She was there, so then I came there.
And I was looking for a job.
And after a couple of months, I got a job and I was working for a big firm.
The firm was [NON-ENGLISH] Export-Import.

We were just getting coffee, tea, all kind of dry foods and chocolate import from Argentina, like all kind of coffees, [NON-ENGLISH] Santos, Rio, all kind of coffees, and then sending out by mail, by train, all over Poland, sending out.
There were 12 people in that place.
Everybody has his station where to take telephone calls and all kind-- so I was like a salesman.
I don't know how to explain it.
But export-import we had.
[NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] was the street from the place in Krakow.
My sister Pepka, no, she had her own apartment.
And I used to stay with my cousin, 'til my mother came there.

But before that-- before that, to describe, before we went out about three years ago-- three years before we moved out, my mother moved out to Krakow, we had a problem when we went on a-- to [NON-ENGLISH].
We went to the other village, to my uncle.
We went to [NON-ENGLISH].
Mom locked up the house and we went there.
She had a lot of jewelry from my grandma and, you know, a lot of jewelry.
She had a chain, gold chain, going three, four times around, my father's jewelry, diamond rings, and all of that.
Some of them she put on on herself.
And a lot of them, she left a whole package, which a lot are my father's jewelry, gold watches and all that.
And so she left it in a place, in a hiding place.
But they broke in.
Somebody broke in through the back door.
They came, broke in.
And they took out all that.
The whole package they found, the jewelry and also money we had.
And we were selling cigarettes.
We were selling cigarettes, because my mother was a widow, so she allowed.
And this was our government.
Not everybody could sell cigarettes or liquor, only a widow or a widower, you know.
So-- and then we came back.
He opened up in the front door and he came in.
We saw the door was open.
The back door was open.
So our mother went right away, oh, somebody broke in.
So there is-- it's nothing there.
It's gone.
So all right.
This was late at night when we came back, so in the morning she went to the priest in the-- in the village.
She went to the priest and she told him the story.
This was I think on a Thursday.
This was on a Thursday.
And she came the next day, Friday.
She came the morning, she went to his private office, right next to his church.
And she told him.
He said, oh, yeah.
He said, don't worry.
He'll get it back.
She said, what do you mean, you'll get it back?
You'll get it back, because I'm going to tell them-- they're going to come to church Sunday.
And they'll come to church.
I'm going to tell them if they don't bring back everything what they stole, the whole package, money, forget it, the cigarettes, you know, it's nothing.
The main thing is your old jewelry what you have from your parents.
You know, it's very-- I know, he say, it's very-- for you, it's very, you know, very important.
So all right.
Then when he came Sunday, they came to church.
And that time, you know, the Ukrainians, they were going to church a lot of-- oh, squadrons going and going.
And they came to the church, so he start praying, the beginning. you know.
And then he came, before he had to say, there's a special prayer.
They call this-- they call that [NON-ENGLISH].
The main thing, the prayer to God, you know.
So he say, I'm going to tell you now something, my people, and it happened so-and-so.
They broke in to-- to this widow, Malka.
They broke in and they took out all her jewelry what she had from the parents and grandparents and all of that stuff.
And it's a very bad thing what your kids do.
So when you come home, you tell them, because I'm not going to say today that [NON-ENGLISH], that special prayer.
I'm not going to say it today.
And I'm not going to say it 'til they'll bring the stuff back.
They don't have to say who it is.
Throw it in.
Put it back.
That's it.
I don't care what you do.
Then, when they went back from the church, going home, they all coming in on our ground, the front-- the front lawn, coming in, and oh, we see [INAUDIBLE].
A couple of hundred people, they all come in.
They all call-- you know.
We came out.
My mother came out.
She say, we didn't know.
You didn't even tell us what's happened, what's happened to you.
But the priest told us he's not going to say the [NON-ENGLISH].
So they say, don't worry.
You'll get it back.
We'll talk to our children.
And our children, whatever who did it, we don't want to tell nobody.
Forget about it, you know, not to put them in jail for that or whatever they did.
They have to bring it back.
And that's what happened.
Monday morning, my mother yet didn't believe so much.
So he-- she said to my older brother, David, she said, David, let's go.
We hire the same man what used to go with my mother sometime to our rabbi to [NON-ENGLISH].
We had a rabbi special, you know, you know, talk a little bit over her problems sometime, you know.
My sister didn't feel good.
She had bad eyes.
And my brother was sick.
He had arthritis.
He fell in once to a well, and this was in the winter.
They went for water with my brother and he fell in.
It was so heavy, and my brother, my other brother, the younger one, left and he-- it pulled him in in the well, deep in the well.
So he was there.
He ran to the neighbor and he told him.
We didn't have the well in our place.
Our neighbor, we took water.
So he says-- he say, come on, my brother fell into the well.
He jumped out.
And he was barefooted yet in the house.
It was in the winter-- and the snow.
And he right away jumped in.
There was like steps going in deep.
And he just pulled up his hand, my brother.
I don't know how he turned even around when he fell in with his head.
And somehow, the water turned him around and he sticked up his hand.
He pulled him out by this hand and brought him-- brought him over to the house, to his house and he put him on the stove.
There was a stove from bricks.
And the stove was very warm and he was cold, so that's why he got sick.
He had-- the arthritis was in every-- every place.
He had terrible arthritis after that.
So-- so that-- this is one story.
So then she say to my brother, let's go to Jaworow.
We went to Jaworow.
There was a man.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], they call him.
[NON-ENGLISH] means 'to look.' He looks for the future, what's going to be or what it was, you know.
So he opened up.
My brother, he say, Mom, I'm not going in.
I don't believe that he can tell you anything.
I don't believe it.
I don't believe it.
So he was staying outside, going someplace.
And he say, you go in.
Later, I'll come back.
So as soon as she came in, she say-- he said, put-- had a book like this, my mother said.
And he opened up something in the book.
I was not there, so I wouldn't know what-- what it say there.
And she put her hand on the book and he started telling her, I know your son doesn't believe in me.
He didn't even want to go in.
And he say, I know your husband got killed in the First World War.
Now I know what you came to me.
You had a big loss.
But when you'll come home, the loss will be already replaced.
You'll have everything, Whatever is there.
So that's it.
She came back home, because that time, there was no telephone to call, to say, you know.
So when she came back home, my-- my sister and my mother left with the front door.
My sister went through the back door to the-- to the cow, to the barn to milk the cows.
She opened the door.
The package was right there, early in the morning, was right there.
She brought it in the house.
So my mother came in.
My sister came out, she said, here, Mom.
Here's the package.
They throw it in.
So that was the story.
But then, three years later, my mother moved away, because she was afraid with kids, you know.
And the young kids were very, very rough and started bothering, so she moved out.
But my grandma was still there with my aunt and uncles.
Only us, we moved out, because we found an-- an apartment for her, so she was selling some of the land, you know.
The forest she sold, and the other land.
But only the three acres she didn't sell.
It's still there.
So we had one man, what he was taking care.
He was a very nice gentle Ukrainian man, very intelligent man.
So he had our land.
Whatever he was working on it, my mother used to come every year and just whatever was taken off, half was taken.
My mother would sell his half, and that's the way it was working.
It's still there, the land.
Only one sister, the middle sister.
Pearl. ?] [?
Pearl. ?] was married.
The name was Jakobowicz.
I got the pictures I got from my sister.
And they all got-- nobody is alive.
I'm the only one.
I'm the only one alive.
So 1937-- first, when I was in Krakow, 1936 they had very bad in Krakow.
Also signs, [NON-ENGLISH], 'beat the Jew,' and this and that, and all kind of things.
So my cousin came.
He was going to the university of Krakow.
This is my cousin from the restaurant.
His name was Geitzholz.
Targets So his oldest son was going to the university.
He come back.
He used to-- they started beating up Jewish students in the university.
He come back all bloody, beat up, the face and the arm here with-- they beat him up, so he came.
Then it started getting really bad.
They said something.
There was a-- a church.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH], very tall, high church.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH].
Somebody from the anti-Semite broke out the window, couple windows in the church.
So they said that the Jews did it.
And that's what they say, making like a pogrom.
So once on a Friday night, I remember like today, I was by my cousin.
And then we hear people running in.
You heard the-- all the students from all over, they're coming on-- we lived on the [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish neighborhood, [NON-ENGLISH].
There was a kosher restaurant, [NON-ENGLISH].
So he say, everybody has to be in the house.
We have-- we're going to have here a bloodbath.
What's going to happen?
They all come.
They want to beat up the Jews.
They want to beat up the Jews.
Before that, they also went to the Jewish university on the Stradom.
There was a Jewish university.
They came.
They want to do something over there, beat up the Jewish kids.
But they throw some-- a lot of hot water.
They throw it on them.
They were all in the-- in the-- on the-- in the guard, you know, before the university.
They were all came there.
They start throwing stones, so they throw hot water on them.
They're burning up and they ran away.
Now, and this wasn't before, a couple of days before.
But then it came on Friday, they supposed to come here.
So the Jewish organization organized the labor, the labor-- the-- the labor organization.
They called it the [NON-ENGLISH], but the labor, the Polish labor front.
They were all going with the Jews.
So they say, we'll help you.
And they'll come.
We'll let them go in in the Jewish neighborhood.
We'll let them go in.
Later, we'll come out from the cellars and we'll block out-- we'll close in, block out all the streets that they can not get out.
And later, they'll come here.
We'll let them go in.
And then we'll come out from the cellars.
We'll come out.
And it was really good organized, saying we're going to give them hell.
And that's the way it happened.
--an area we had three brothers.
We called them the Glazman brothers.
They were very-- always with the police, but the police was afraid of them.
They were so quick.
They were so-- so strong and everything.
And they say they organized with the Glazman brothers also.
So it happened.
They came in.
They blocked them out.
They came out from the-- they start beating up with-- with sticks, with all kind of things, beat up.
All the sudden, they have to call in ambulances all to take them out.
And that's quiet down.
For a while, it quieted down.
You were there?
I-- I was-- no.
I didn't go out.
I wouldn't go out, because first of all, I didn't live long in Krakow.
I didn't know.
But people, they were organized, young people they were in Krakow.
They were really good organized.
And they did a good job.
And then, it's also-- it's it started going worse and worse.
But then 1937, they took me in the Polish Army.
My mother was still--
Well, this happened, and the thoughts was, I had family.
We had family in the United States.
And they couldn't do something to send us-- you know.
One of my brothers, one of the other uncles, a son or a daughter, take them to the United States.
It started getting so bad.
One uncle couldn't afford it.
But I don't know.
He was busy.
He was in the catering business.
He was always busy.
And he didn't think of it, and that's the way.
We were thinking that somebody would take us out, somebody would take us out to the United States.
But it didn't happen.
So then when they took me in the Army already, he couldn't do nothing.
I was in the Army.
But he actually maybe would take me, the youngest one.
Oh, in the beginning of 1937.
I don't know-- I don't-- I don't remember exactly the date.
The beginning 1937.
My relatives?
My mother was still in the village.
My whole family, one-- the whole family was still in the villages.
Still in the villages, living in the villages.
But later, in 1938, my mother came already, because my sister found already the apartment and everything.
And she starts selling whatever she could, liquidating the whole house and everything.
And then she came with my older sister and my two brothers.
First of all, my middle brother, Jakob, my mother gave him to learn-- he didn't go too much to school.
He didn't like too much to school.
And my mother couldn't afford to hire a special somebody to come to teach him.
So anyway, she took him in a city, [NON-ENGLISH], friends, that he should learn a trade.
So he learned for three years.
So he went to learn a trade, to paint, a house painter.
Back in Poland, the house painter was specialist.
They have to know-- with sketches, one on top of the other, three, four sketches and later come out clouds and trees and all kinds of things, really beautiful job.
So he learned that trade.
And when he came to Krakow, he had a very good job.
He was making very good money in Krakow.
This is my-- my middle brother.
And now it's 1937.
Could you describe how you were made to go with the Polish Army?
Well, '37, they call me to the Polish Army.
I was by the light artillery.
And [NON-ENGLISH] means the 22nd polk from the-- they called the name was the [NON-ENGLISH].
We had feathers on our-- here.
We had feathers on the side, on the cap.
And I-- the first six months, you know, you exercise, going out, little maneuvers here and there.
Yes, I was drafted.
Yes, I was drafted, a year before.
I was drafted a year before.
But then, a year later, they took me in the Army.
So in the Polish Army, I had very good.
I was really-- tried to be, you know, like they say, a Jew doesn't like to go in the Polish Army.
People were paying off who can.
Some people made themselves sick.
Years ago, they cut even fingers off, you know.
And they-- they didn't want to go to the Army.
It was strict.
Polish Army was very strict.
Even they won the war in three weeks, but the uniforms and everything was so spotless, clean, nice.
And the exercises, very rough, very rough.
But for me, it didn't mean nothing, because I was always not afraid of nothing, jumping or high or far.
I was always-- made-- made the norm better than somebody else.
And I liked to ride the horses, so instead of going to the machinery, to the cannons, I wanted to go to the horses.
And it was not easy by the horses, but I loved it.
So then I went to the horses and I was riding horses, and also, not only riding, I was making-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH].
In Poland, [NON-ENGLISH] means you have tricks.
On the horses, you make tricks, all kinds of tricks, like you jump on the horse on the saddle from the back, jump on the saddle, cutting trees.
They had trees put up, how many trees, so many hat you cut, you know, like-- actually, the caballeria used to be-- my cousin used to be in the caballeria, was strictly only horses, no cavalry at all.
So this, who cuts more, it's-- they give you a A, a B, whatever, you know.
So-- and then when it came to the jumping and there was an inspection came from the higher rank, came an inspection and they was watching.
And we were doing it.
And instead of jumping on the horse, I jumped over the head on the other side, over the horse on the other side.
And that's it.
And that's when he called in my captain.
He called in my captain.
He tell him, can I know the name from this soldier?
He said, yes.
This is Leon Scher.
He called me over.
And he says, you did a beautiful job.
But I didn't see anybody else should do that, he said.
So he gave me two weeks to go home, a vacation.
Yes.
This was also happened to my brothers.
My brothers were also in the Army before me.
Soon, he came.
They didn't recognize my father's name, Spirer.
They recognized because my grandma.
And my grandpa didn't married in court.
They married only in like the Jewish religion.
And they didn't recognize the name Spirer, my father's, or my grandfather's name.
My grandma's name, so we were going on my grandma's name was Scher.
And that's the way we-- all the boys, all Scher after the army, all our documents, everything, started at Scher.
That's the way it was going 'til now.
But originally, we were Spirer, 'til the-- 'til the Polish Army.
My brother was drafted.
He was the sick man.
He was drafted.
My mother went to lawyers, to everything to do something not to let him go in the Army.
He can't do the exercise.
So then it didn't help, nothing.
He went to the Army.
And he didn't lasted too long.
He start failing.
He couldn't run.
He couldn't do it. 'Til after three months they let him go home, so they freed him.
But my other brother, second brother was a painter.
He was-- he was in the-- in the infantry.
Infantry.
Jakob, yeah.
He was two years.
Then I was-- I was two years, 'til the war broke out.
I had yet three to four weeks-- three to four weeks to be in the army.
But then the war broke out in 1939.
The war broke out, so that's it.
I didn't make no more-- I didn't come back to Krakow, no more to my house.
I went to my family in [NON-ENGLISH], in the villages.
And then I was-- I had an apartment in Jaworow.
Oh, yes.
There were a lot of-- a lot of people from [NON-ENGLISH].
This is over close to the German borders, like [NON-ENGLISH].
There were a lot of [NON-ENGLISH], half-Polish, half-Jewish-- half-German, mixed marriages.
So they were very anti-Semitic.
They always used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], you Jew, [NON-ENGLISH].
So I used to tell them off.
I'd say, you know, you're gonna-- you're gonna-- you're gonna clean my boots yet.
You know, that dirty Jew, you're going to clean my boots.
So he'd says, what?
What?
What?
Later, when I had a good job-- I was working in the office after the six months.
I was working in the office and I was allowed also to wear hair, not to shave the hair no more, because all the two years, you can't wear no hair.
But I was allowed by my captain to wear the hair.
And then I worked in the office.
I was writing the reports every day, the exercise what has to be done, you know, on a big board, writing down every day what's going to be done.
Sometimes the captain and the-- the first lieutenant couldn't go out to call the captain, you know, to the report.
I used to put the soldiers-- used to put them to their report and give them salute and [INAUDIBLE], all the soldiers clean, all the soldiers are ready to the report.
So that's what-- and I became a little bit-- a little bit loved in my-- in my polk where I was.
I used to-- and then they gave me the magazine from the uniforms I should take care, and also the rifle magazine to look at over, which one is clean, which one is not clean.
So once this guy, this anti-Semite came over, he had to come to my office first.
For they wanted to go out to the city, they have to come to my office and see for to give him a-- a-- you know, a paper that they can go out to the city.
And now we have to take a pause to change tapes.
